content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 7, 1993

Bradley W. Sullivan
City Attorney
City of Sutter Creek
Dummit, Faber, & Brown
1661 Garden Highway
Sacramento, CA  95833-9706

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-93-078

Dear Mr. Sullivan:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Heidi Bollwow regarding her responsibilities under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.  In
addition, the Commission has no jurisdiction over other laws that
may apply to your facts, such as Section 1090.  As we stated in
our letter of February 22, 1993, Section 1090 is not a part of the
Act, and therefore the Commission has no jurisdiction over this
provision.  You should contact the Attorney General's Office
regarding Section 1090 questions.


_____________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA  95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-078
Page 2


QUESTION

Does Councilmember Boitano have a conflict of interest by
virtue of a contractual relationship between the city and the
Boitano Family Trust?

CONCLUSION

So long as Councilmember Boitano does not participate in
decisions affecting the parking lot or the Boitano Family Trust,
the councilmember will be acting consistent with the conflict of
interest requirements of the Act.

FACTS

In 1976, the City of Sutter Creek entered into a lease of
property owned by John Perrecio and Maurice Boitano (Councilmember
Boitano's father) to be used as a parking lot. The property was
placed in the Boitano Family Trust in 1988. You stated that
Councilmember Heidi Boitano is a beneficiary and trustee of the
Boitano Family Trust.

Since that time, the surface of the lot has deteriorated.
Due to citizen and neighbor complaints, a new agreement was
drafted between the city and the owners to deal with the costs of
resurfacing the lot. You anticipate future decisions will arise
regarding the agreement and the trust property.

ANALYSIS

You stated that Councilmember Boitano, is a beneficiary and
trustee of the Boitano Family Trust.2 Since 1976, the Boitano
Family Trust has leased real property to the city for use as a
parking lot. However, you also stated that Councilmember Boitano
has abstained from participating in any city decisions pertaining
to the lot.

You have asked whether by virtue of the contract relationship
between the city and the trust, Councilmember Boitano would have a
conflict of interest under the Act.

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using the official's
position to influence a governmental decision in which the of-
ficial has a financial interest. A "public official" is defined
in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who
is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. This definition would include a member of the
Sutter Creek City Council.

2
You also mentioned a former councilmember, Mr. Louis Boitano.
However, Mr. Boitano is not a councilmember at this time. Thus,
the analysis in this letter has no bearing on Mr. Louis Boitano.

Our File No. A-93-078
Page 3


     Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of the official's immediate family or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     (e)  Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

     For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

     You stated that Councilmember Boitano has an interest in the
parking lot in question because she is a beneficiary of the

Our File No. A-93-078
Page 4


Boitano Family Trust.³ However, the existence of an economic
interest, in itself, does not create a conflict of interest. In
addition to other factors, the official must be making,
participating in making, or otherwise using the official's
position to influence the governmental decision that will affect
the official's economic interest.

What constitutes "participation" in a governmental decision
is broadly defined and can include voting, conducting research or
making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment
on the part of the official or designated employee or contacts or
appearances before any member, officer, employee or consultant of
the agency. (Regulations 18700 and 18700.1, copies enclosed.)

You stated that Councilmember Boitano has not, and will not
participate in decisions affecting the parking lot or the Boitano
family Trust. Under such circumstances, the councilmember would
not have a conflict of interest by virtue of the trust interest in
the parking lot.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁴

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division


3    Please note that because we do not have the full trust document
we have not analyzed the nature of the economic interest.
Instead, we are relying on your facts as to the interest of
Councilmember Boitano. The Commission does not act as the finder
of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
71.) However, we have included Regulation 18234 for your
information.

4    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

QUESTION: Does Councilmember Boitano have a conflict of interest by
virtue of a contractual relationship between the city and the
Boitano Family Trust?

CONCLUSION: So long as Councilinember Boitano does not participate in
decisions affecting the parking lot or the Boitano Family Trust,
the councilmember will be acting consistent with the conflict of
interest requirements of the Act.
